notes.text: WORKFLOW: group phase comparison. The canonical way to ask 'do these two groups peak at different times of day?'. Step 1 — each group's subjects are fitted by one Cosinor node; because the scalar metric ports report ONE VALUE PER Y INPUT, the node's `acrophase` port emits a whole column of per-subject peak times (h), i.e. that group's phase distribution. Step 2 — both acrophase columns are wired into the circular phase plot, placing each subject on a 24 h clock with its group's Rayleigh mean-resultant vector (arrow length R: 0 = dispersed, 1 = tightly clustered). Step 3 — the same two columns feed the Rayleigh test node, which always reports R, z and a p-value per group (is there a preferred direction at all?) and, with the Watson-Williams toggle on, additionally reports F and a p-value for whether the groups share a common mean direction — the circular analogue of one-way ANOVA. Read it as: significant Rayleigh = each group is genuinely clustered; significant Watson-Williams = the groups peak at different phases. Swap the Cosinor node for a periodogram peak or an actogram onset marker to phase-type the same way (Batschelet 1981; Watson & Williams 1956; Cornelissen 2014).
